era_context: The mid-1820s sit at the intersection of three transformative currents: the final collapse of Spanish colonial authority in the Americas, the consolidation of post-Napoleonic conservative order in Europe under the Concert of Europe, and the accelerating spread of industrial capitalism centred on Britain. The Greek War of Independence continued to destabilise the Ottoman Empire and galvanise Romantic nationalism across Europe. In Southeast Asia, the British and Dutch were formalising imperial spheres of influence, while the British East India Company expanded aggressively into Burma. Across the Atlantic, the newly independent Latin American republics were experimenting with constitutional governance.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal zones and Ethiopia/Zulu kingdom
Inner Central Asia (Khanates of Bukhara, Khiva, Kokand)
Non-elite social history across all regions

events.title: Battle of Ayacucho Ends Spanish Rule in South America
events.description: On December 9, 1824, a patriot army of approximately 5,800 under General Antonio José de Sucre decisively defeated the royalist forces of Viceroy José de la Serna at Ayacucho in the Peruvian highlands. The royalists, numbering around 9,300, suffered a catastrophic collapse; La Serna himself was wounded and captured. The resulting Capitulation of Ayacucho ended effective Spanish military authority on the South American mainland. The battle was the culmination of Bolívar's campaign to liberate Peru and represented the final major engagement of the South American wars of independence that had raged since 1810.
key_figures: Simón Bolívar (dictator of Peru, overall campaign leader)
José de la Serna (Spanish Viceroy of Peru)
José de Canterac (royalist general, signed capitulation)

sources.name: Capitulation of Ayacucho (signed December 9, 1824); Sucre's official dispatches to Bolívar
John Lynch, Simón Bolívar: A Life (Yale, 2006); Timothy Anna, The Fall of the Royal Government in Peru (Nebraska, 1979)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both patriot and royalist military records, diplomatic correspondence, and contemporary press. Date secure.

events.title: Battle of Junín
events.description: On August 6, 1824, Bolívar's cavalry force defeated a royalist cavalry contingent at the Battle of Junín on the high plateau of central Peru. The engagement, fought almost entirely with swords and lances with virtually no gunfire, lasted approximately 45 minutes but was strategically decisive: it shattered royalist morale and opened the road to the southern highlands where the war would be decided at Ayacucho four months later. Junín demonstrated Bolívar's ability to campaign effectively at altitudes above 4,000 metres.
key_figures: José de Canterac (royalist commander at Junín)
Isidoro Suárez (patriot cavalry officer, led decisive charge)

sources.name: Bolívar's campaign dispatches and correspondence, 1824; Daniel Florencio O'Leary, Memorias (published posthumously, 1879–1888)
John Lynch, The Spanish American Revolutions, 1808–1826 (Norton, 1986)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented by multiple participant accounts. Date and location secure.

events.title: First Anglo-Burmese War Begins
events.description: In March 1824, the British East India Company declared war on the Konbaung dynasty of Burma following Burmese incursions into Assam, Manipur, and Arakan — frontier regions contested between the expanding Burmese and British Indian empires. A British expeditionary force under Sir Archibald Campbell landed at Rangoon in May 1824, beginning a gruelling two-year campaign. The war, the longest and most expensive of Britain's Indian wars to that date, would end in 1826 with the Treaty of Yandabo, stripping Burma of significant territory and beginning its slow subjugation. For Burma, the war marked the beginning of the end of the Konbaung dynasty's independence.

sources.name: East India Company records and parliamentary papers on the Burma war; Henry Havelock, Memoir of the Three Campaigns of Major-General Sir Archibald Campbell's Army in Ava (1828)
Thant Myint-U, The Making of Modern Burma (Cambridge, 2001); G. P. Ramachandra, 'The Outbreak of the First Anglo-Burmese War,' Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society (1978)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in British official records, parliamentary debates, and Burmese chronicles (Hmannan Yazawin). Dates secure.

events.title: Anglo-Dutch Treaty Divides Southeast Asia
events.description: On March 17, 1824, Britain and the Netherlands signed the Treaty of London, resolving decades of commercial and territorial friction in Southeast Asia. Britain ceded its trading posts in Sumatra (including Bencoolen) and recognised Dutch supremacy over the entire Indonesian archipelago; the Netherlands ceded Malacca and its Indian possessions and recognised British control of Singapore. The treaty drew an invisible line through the Strait of Malacca that would define the colonial — and eventually national — boundaries of modern Malaysia, Singapore, and Indonesia. It formalised the partition of the Malay world between two European empires.
key_figures: Stamford Raffles (founder of Singapore, whose position the treaty secured)

sources.name: Treaty between His Britannic Majesty and the King of the Netherlands, signed March 17, 1824 (text in British and Foreign State Papers, vol. XI)
Nicholas Tarling, Anglo-Dutch Rivalry in the Malay World 1780–1824 (Cambridge, 1962); C. M. Turnbull, A History of Modern Singapore, 1819–2005 (NUS Press, 2009)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in multiple archives. Date and terms are undisputed.

events.title: Death of Lord Byron at Missolonghi
events.description: On April 19, 1824, the English Romantic poet George Gordon, Lord Byron, died of fever at Missolonghi in western Greece, where he had gone to support the Greek War of Independence. Byron had arrived in January 1824, committing personal funds and attempting to unite fractious Greek factions. His death at age 36 transformed him into a martyr for the Greek cause and electrified European philhellenic sentiment, significantly increasing foreign sympathy, volunteer recruitment, and financial support for the Greek insurgency. His death became one of the defining moments of European Romanticism and helped shift public opinion toward intervention.

sources.name: Pietro Gamba, A Narrative of Lord Byron's Last Journey to Greece (1825); Byron's letters and journals
Fiona MacCarthy, Byron: Life and Legend (Faber, 2002); William St Clair, That Greece Might Still Be Free: The Philhellenes in the War of Independence (Oxford, 1972; repr. Open Book, 2008)

events.certainty_note: Date, location, and circumstances documented in extensive contemporary correspondence and memoir accounts.

events.title: Premiere of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony
events.description: On May 7, 1824, Ludwig van Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 in D minor, Op. 125, premiered at the Kärntnertortheater in Vienna. The work, composed while Beethoven was profoundly deaf, introduced vocal soloists and chorus into the symphonic form for the first time in a major work, setting Friedrich Schiller's 'Ode to Joy' in its final movement. The audience responded with wild enthusiasm — Beethoven, unable to hear the applause, had to be turned around by the contralto Caroline Unger to see it. The Ninth Symphony became one of the most influential compositions in Western music history and its 'Ode to Joy' melody was later adopted as the anthem of the European Union.
key_figures: Michael Umlauf (conductor who directed the orchestra)

sources.name: Contemporary Viennese press reviews (Wiener Allgemeine musikalische Zeitung, 1824); Anton Schindler, Biographie von Ludwig van Beethoven (1840, revised 1860)
Jan Swafford, Beethoven: Anguish and Triumph (Houghton Mifflin Harcourt, 2014); Maynard Solomon, Beethoven (Schirmer, 1977; rev. 1998)

events.certainty_note: Premiere date, venue, and reception documented in multiple contemporary accounts and press reviews.

events.title: Mexico Adopts Federal Republican Constitution
events.description: On October 4, 1824, Mexico promulgated its first federal republican constitution, establishing the United Mexican States as a federal republic of 19 states and 4 territories with a bicameral congress, an elected president, and Catholicism as the state religion. The constitution drew on both the U.S. Constitution and the Spanish Constitution of 1812. Guadalupe Victoria was inaugurated as the first president on October 10. The constitution represented a compromise between federalists and centralists, but the underlying tension between these factions would dominate Mexican politics for decades, contributing to instability, coups, and eventually the Reform War.
key_figures: Miguel Ramos Arizpe (chief architect of the constitution)
Servando Teresa de Mier (congressman, federalist advocate)

sources.name: Constitución Federal de los Estados Unidos Mexicanos (October 4, 1824); Actas del Congreso Constituyente
Timothy Anna, Forging Mexico, 1821–1835 (Nebraska, 1998); Jaime E. Rodríguez O., 'The Constitution of 1824 and the Formation of the Mexican State,' in The Divine Charter (Rowman & Littlefield, 2005)

events.certainty_note: Constitution text and congressional debates survive in full. All dates secure.

events.title: United States Presidential Election Deadlock
events.description: The presidential election of 1824 was the first in which the popular vote was widely recorded and the first to produce no Electoral College majority. Four candidates — Andrew Jackson, John Quincy Adams, William Crawford, and Henry Clay — all ran as nominal Democratic-Republicans, signalling the collapse of the first party system. Jackson won the most popular and electoral votes but fell short of a majority, throwing the election to the House of Representatives (decided in February 1825). Clay's subsequent support for Adams, who won in the House, and his appointment as Secretary of State, led to Jackson's furious accusation of a 'corrupt bargain' that galvanised the emergence of Jacksonian democracy.
key_figures: Henry Clay (candidate, Speaker of the House)
William H. Crawford (candidate, Secretary of the Treasury)

sources.name: Official returns, state canvass records, and congressional proceedings (Annals of Congress, 18th Congress)
Daniel Walker Howe, What Hath God Wrought: The Transformation of America, 1815–1848 (Oxford, 2007); Robert V. Remini, Andrew Jackson and the Course of American Freedom (Harper & Row, 1981)

events.certainty_note: Electoral returns and contemporary correspondence survive in full. All dates and vote counts secure.

events.title: Sadi Carnot Publishes Reflections on the Motive Power of Fire
events.description: In 1824, Nicolas Léonard Sadi Carnot published Réflexions sur la puissance motrice du feu et sur les machines propres à développer cette puissance, a short treatise that laid the theoretical foundations of thermodynamics. Carnot demonstrated that the efficiency of a heat engine depends only on the temperature difference between its hot and cold reservoirs, not on the working substance — a principle now known as the Carnot cycle. The work went largely unnoticed during Carnot's lifetime (he died in 1832 at age 36) but was rediscovered by Émile Clapeyron in the 1830s and became foundational for the work of Clausius, Kelvin, and the entire field of thermodynamics.
key_figures: Nicolas Léonard Sadi Carnot (physicist and engineer)

sources.name: Sadi Carnot, Réflexions sur la puissance motrice du feu (Bachelier, Paris, 1824)
Robert Fox, The Caloric Theory of Gases from Lavoisier to Regnault (Oxford, 1971); D. S. L. Cardwell, From Watt to Clausius (Cornell, 1971)

events.certainty_note: Published work survives in full. Publication date secure.

events.title: National Gallery Founded in London
events.description: In 1824, the British Parliament voted to purchase 38 paintings from the estate of the recently deceased banker John Julius Angerstein for £57,000, establishing the core collection of the National Gallery. The collection was initially displayed at Angerstein's former townhouse at 100 Pall Mall. Unlike continental equivalents such as the Louvre, which originated from royal collections, the National Gallery was conceived as a public institution from the outset, intended for the education and enjoyment of all social classes. The purpose-built gallery in Trafalgar Square would not open until 1838.
key_figures: George IV (King of the United Kingdom)

sources.name: House of Commons debates and Treasury records relating to the Angerstein purchase, 1824; contemporary newspaper coverage
Jonathan Conlin, The Nation's Mantelpiece: A History of the National Gallery (Pallas Athene, 2006)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary vote, purchase price, and collection details fully documented.

events.title: Repeal of the Combination Acts in Britain
events.description: In June 1824, Parliament repealed the Combination Acts of 1799–1800, which had prohibited trade unions and collective bargaining by workers. The repeal was largely engineered by the radical tailor Francis Place and the MP Joseph Hume, who manoeuvred it through a sparsely attended Parliament. The immediate result was an explosion of trade union activity and strikes, which alarmed employers and led to the partially restrictive Combination Act of 1825 — but the fundamental right of workers to combine was preserved. The 1824 repeal is considered a landmark moment in British labour history and the history of organised labour worldwide.

sources.name: Parliamentary debates (Hansard), 1824; Report of the Select Committee on Artizans and Machinery, 1824
E. P. Thompson, The Making of the English Working Class (Gollancz, 1963); Iorwerth Prothero, Artisans and Politics in Early Nineteenth-Century London (Dawson, 1979)

events.certainty_note: Legislation and parliamentary debates survive in full. Date secure.

events.title: Joseph Aspdin Patents Portland Cement
events.description: On October 21, 1824, Joseph Aspdin, a bricklayer from Leeds, was granted British Patent No. 5022 for a process he called 'Portland Cement' — named because the set material resembled Portland stone. Aspdin's process involved heating a mixture of finely ground limestone and clay in a kiln, then grinding the resulting clinker into powder. While Aspdin's original product was not fired at the high temperatures used in modern Portland cement (which was improved by his son William and by Isaac Johnson in the 1840s), the patent marks the beginning of the modern cement industry that would transform global construction in the industrial age.

sources.name: British Patent No. 5022 (October 21, 1824), 'An Improvement in the Modes of Producing an Artificial Stone'
A. J. Francis, The Cement Industry 1796–1914: A History (David & Charles, 1977)

events.certainty_note: Patent document survives. Date secure. However, the product Aspdin actually patented differed from modern Portland cement — see disconfirming evidence.

events.title: Siege of Missolonghi Continues; Greek Independence War Intensifies
events.description: Throughout 1824, the Greek War of Independence entered a chaotic phase characterised by inter-factional civil war among the Greeks alongside continued fighting against Ottoman forces. The First Greek Civil War erupted between the government faction under Georgios Kountouriotis and the military chieftains of the Morea. Despite internal divisions, Greek naval forces — particularly from the islands of Hydra, Spetses, and Psara — continued to challenge Ottoman maritime control. The Ottomans destroyed the island of Psara in June 1824, massacring or enslaving much of the population, an atrocity that further galvanised European philhellenic opinion.
key_figures: Georgios Kountouriotis (president of the Greek provisional government)

sources.name: Dispatches from European consuls and agents in the Levant; contemporary philhellenic periodicals
David Brewer, The Greek War of Independence (Overlook, 2001); Richard Clogg, A Concise History of Greece (Cambridge, 3rd ed., 2013)

events.certainty_note: Events documented by multiple contemporary European observers. Specific casualty figures for Psara are disputed but the event itself is well-attested.

events.title: Ranjit Singh Consolidates the Sikh Empire
events.description: By 1824, Maharaja Ranjit Singh's Sikh Empire had reached its mature phase, controlling the Punjab, Kashmir, and territories extending to the Khyber Pass. Ranjit Singh continued military modernisation, employing European officers — particularly Jean-François Allard and Jean-Baptiste Ventura, who arrived around this period — to train his forces on European lines. The Khalsa army became one of the most formidable military forces in Asia. Ranjit Singh maintained a careful diplomatic balance with the British East India Company to the south, having signed the Treaty of Amritsar (1809) which fixed the Sutlej as the boundary between Sikh and British spheres.
key_figures: Maharaja Ranjit Singh (ruler of the Sikh Empire)

sources.name: British political agent reports from Ludhiana; Sikh court records (various Gurmukhi and Persian sources)
J. S. Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (Cambridge, 1990; rev. 1998); Khushwant Singh, Ranjit Singh: Maharaja of the Punjab (Orient Longman, 1962; rev. 2001)

events.certainty_note: Empire's status well-documented in British, Sikh, and European sources. Exact timing of some European officers' arrival is approximate (±1–2 years).

events.title: Shaka kaSenzangakhona Expands the Zulu Kingdom
events.description: By 1824, King Shaka had consolidated the Zulu kingdom into the dominant power in southeastern Africa through military innovation (the iklwa short stabbing spear, the 'horns of the buffalo' formation) and the incorporation or destruction of neighbouring chiefdoms. In 1824, a party of British traders led by Francis Farewell and Henry Fynn arrived at Port Natal (Durban) and established contact with Shaka, beginning a complex relationship. Fynn's diaries, though written retrospectively and shaped by colonial perspectives, remain a key (if problematic) source for this period. The mfecane/difaqane — the chain of displacement, migration, and state formation radiating outward from Zulu expansion — was reshaping populations across a vast area of southern and central Africa.

sources.name: Henry Francis Fynn, The Diary of Henry Francis Fynn (ed. Stuart & Malcolm, 1950; written retrospectively, c. 1830s–1850s)

Carolyn Hamilton, Terrific Majesty: The Powers of Shaka Zulu and the Limits of Historical Invention (Harvard, 1998); John Wright, 'Turbulent Times: Political Transformations in the North and East, 1760s–1830s,' in Cambridge History of South Africa, vol. 1 (2010)

events.certainty_note: The broad outline of Zulu expansion under Shaka is well-established, but precise chronology of specific events depends on retrospective European accounts and oral traditions. The scale and nature of the mfecane is actively debated in modern scholarship.

events.title: Daoguang Emperor's Second Year; Qing Dynasty Governance
events.description: In 1824 (Daoguang 4th year), the Qing Empire under the Daoguang Emperor faced growing fiscal and administrative challenges. The opium trade through Canton continued to expand despite imperial prohibitions, draining silver from the economy and creating a growing balance-of-payments problem. The Grand Canal and Yellow River flooding posed persistent governance challenges. Daoguang, known for his personal frugality, attempted fiscal retrenchment but struggled with systemic corruption and the vast scale of the empire's bureaucratic apparatus. China remained the world's largest economy but was entering a period of relative decline.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty); Grand Council memorials
William Rowe, China's Last Empire: The Great Qing (Harvard, 2009); James Polachek, The Inner Opium War (Harvard, 1992)

events.certainty_note: Qing governmental records for this period are extensive. Specific policy details documented in official memorials and edicts.

events.title: Federation of Central America Established
events.description: On November 22, 1824, the Federal Republic of Central America adopted its constitution, formally uniting Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica as a single federal state modelled partly on the United States. The republic emerged from the dissolution of the short-lived Mexican Empire of Iturbide (1822–1823). Manuel José Arce was elected the first president. The federation was plagued from the start by rivalry between liberals and conservatives, between Guatemala City and the other states, and by weak federal authority. It would progressively disintegrate during the 1830s and formally dissolve by 1841.
key_figures: José Cecilio del Valle (losing presidential candidate, leading intellectual)

sources.name: Constitución de la República Federal de Centro-América (November 22, 1824)
Ralph Lee Woodward Jr., Central America: A Nation Divided (Oxford, 3rd ed., 1999); Jordana Dym, From Sovereign Villages to National States (New Mexico, 2006)

events.certainty_note: Constitution and congressional records survive. Dates secure.

events.title: Liberia: American Colonization Society Settlement Expanded
events.description: In 1824, the American Colonization Society's settlement in West Africa was formally named Liberia (from Latin liber, 'free'), and its main settlement was named Monrovia after U.S. President James Monroe. Under the governance of Jehudi Ashmun, the colony survived precarious early years marked by conflict with indigenous Dei and Bassa peoples, disease, and supply shortages. The colony represented the deeply ambiguous project of 'returning' free Black Americans and manumitted enslaved people to Africa — supported by both genuine abolitionists and slaveholders who wanted to remove free Black people from the United States.
key_figures: Lott Carey (Baptist minister, early settler and leader)

sources.name: American Colonization Society annual reports and correspondence; Jehudi Ashmun's dispatches

Claude Clegg, The Price of Liberty: African Americans and the Making of Liberia (UNC Press, 2004); James Ciment, Another America: The Story of Liberia and the Former Slaves Who Ruled It (Hill and Wang, 2013)

events.certainty_note: ACS records are detailed. The exact date of the naming 'Liberia' in 1824 is attested in ACS records.

events.title: Russo-American Treaty Limits Claims in Northwest America
events.description: On April 17, 1824 (April 5, Old Style), Russia and the United States signed a convention in St. Petersburg establishing 54°40' North as the southern limit of Russian territorial claims on the Pacific coast of North America. The treaty resolved tensions arising from Tsar Alexander I's ukase of 1821, which had claimed exclusive Russian rights down to 51° North and a 100-mile maritime exclusion zone. The agreement opened the coast to both nations' shipping and effectively confined Russian America to what would become Alaska. A similar Anglo-Russian convention followed in 1825.

sources.name: Convention between the United States of America and His Majesty the Emperor of All the Russias, April 17, 1824 (U.S. Treaties and Other International Agreements)
Howard Kushner, Conflict on the Northwest Coast (Greenwood, 1975); Ilya Vinkovetsky, Russian America: An Overseas Colony of a Continental Empire, 1804–1867 (Oxford, 2011)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives in multiple archives. Date secure.

events.title: Charles X Succeeds to the French Throne
events.description: On September 16, 1824, King Louis XVIII of France died, and his brother the Comte d'Artois succeeded as Charles X. The new king was an ultra-royalist and devout Catholic who openly rejected the compromises of the constitutional Charter of 1814. His accession signalled a rightward shift in French politics: Charles would pursue clerical and aristocratic restoration, compensate émigrés for Revolutionary-era confiscations, and attempt to curtail press freedom. These policies would progressively alienate the bourgeoisie and liberal opposition, culminating in the July Revolution of 1830 that toppled him.

sources.name: Moniteur Universel and contemporary French press coverage, September 1824; diplomatic dispatches
Guillaume de Bertier de Sauvigny, The Bourbon Restoration (Pennsylvania, 1966); Munro Price, The Perilous Crown: France Between Revolutions (Macmillan, 2007)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances fully documented in official and diplomatic records.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed merit qualification. (1) Joseph Aspdin's 1824 patent (1824-012): Modern materials scientists have established that Aspdin's original 'Portland cement' was fired at temperatures too low to produce true clinker — the key chemical process (sintering at ~1450°C) that defines modern Portland cement was likely achieved by Isaac Johnson in the 1840s and commercially perfected by Aspdin's son William. The 1824 patent is thus better understood as a precursor rather than the invention of Portland cement as used today. (2) The mfecane/difaqane (1824-015): The traditional narrative of Shaka as sole driver of a catastrophic wave of destruction across southern Africa has been substantially revised since the 1980s. Julian Cobbing and others have argued that European slave-trading and colonial disruption were equal or greater causes of population displacement, and that the 'mfecane' concept was partly constructed by colonial historians to justify land seizures by attributing depopulation to African agency. Carolyn Hamilton's work occupies a middle position, acknowledging Zulu expansion while critiquing the exaggerated scale of destruction attributed to Shaka. (3) The 1824 U.S. election (1824-008): The 'corrupt bargain' narrative — that Clay traded his support for Adams in exchange for the Secretary of State appointment — is strongly disputed by some historians (e.g., Mary Hargreaves), who argue Clay had ideological reasons to prefer Adams over Jackson regardless of patronage. No documentary evidence of an explicit quid pro quo has been found. (4) Carnot's work (1824-009): The publication initially had almost no impact; fewer than a few hundred copies were printed. Its significance was entirely retrospective, recognised only after Clapeyron's 1834 reformulation.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1824 is rich and multi-sourced for Europe, the Americas, and British India, drawn from governmental archives, parliamentary records, diplomatic correspondence, and a robust periodical press. For sub-Saharan Africa, sources are almost entirely retrospective European accounts (Fynn's diary, missionary reports) and oral traditions recorded decades later — these are filtered through colonial perspectives and must be used critically. For China, the Qing Veritable Records provide detailed governmental documentation, but non-elite social history remains thin. Southeast Asian history for this period relies heavily on European colonial records, with indigenous perspectives underrepresented. The overall bias of surviving sources privileges literate, urban, governmental perspectives across all regions.

graph_edges.note: Ayacucho was the culminating battle of the Peruvian independence struggle declared in 1821
Sucre's victory at Ayacucho led directly to the creation of Bolivia (named for Bolívar) in 1825
The First Anglo-Burmese War ended with the Treaty of Yandabo in 1826
The Anglo-Dutch Treaty resolved the diplomatic crisis created by Raffles's founding of Singapore in 1819
Byron's death intensified European philhellenic sentiment, contributing to the eventual armed intervention at Navarino in 1827
The 1824 electoral deadlock was resolved in the House in February 1825, producing the 'corrupt bargain' controversy
Jackson's supporters' fury over 1824 fuelled his victorious 1828 campaign and the rise of the Democratic Party
Carnot's 1824 work, rediscovered by Clapeyron (1834), became foundational for Clausius's formulation of the second law of thermodynamics
The Greek civil war of 1824 weakened the independence movement, contributing to the fall of Missolonghi in 1826
The Central American Federation emerged from the collapse of Iturbide's Mexican Empire in 1823
The Russo-American convention implemented the Monroe Doctrine's principle of excluding new European colonisation in the Americas
Charles X's ultra-royalist policies, beginning at his 1824 accession, provoked the July Revolution of 1830
